coredata.dc:title: Adsorption of carbon dots onto Al2O3 in aqueous: Experimental and theoretical studies
coredata.dc:creator: Liu, Xia
Li, Jiaxing
Wu, Xiaohua
Zeng, Zhi
Wang, Xianlong
Hayat, Tasawar
Zhang, Xiaodong
coredata.dc:description: Increasing interests in carbon dots (CDs) research resulted in the increased production of CDs and the risk to be released into the environment, including surface water and groundwater. To assess the environmental behavior of CDs, the stability of CDs in aqueous solutions were first examined by different environmental conditions, followed by the systematic investigation of the adsorption behaviors of CDs onto Al2O3 surface. Electrostatic interactions were considered as the dominant forces in CDs adsorption onto Al2O3 process, which were further confirmed by controlling experiments as compared with other minerals or oxides, such as montmorillonite, kaolinite and SiO2. Theoretical calculations and characterization of Al2O3/CDs revealed chemisorptions exist in the adsorption process. Our investigation results provided fundamental understanding towards the interaction of CDs with Al2O3 surface, and potential fate of CDs under natural conditions in aquatic environment, as well as in soils and sediments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Al2O3
Adsorption
Electrostatic interactions

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Stability of CDs under different environmental conditions
Adsorption isotherm for CDs on Al2O3
Interactions mechanism at the CDs-Al2O3 surface

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of CDs. XRD spectrum of CDs(A) FT-IR spectrum of CDs(B) TEM of CDs(C) UV-vis spectrum of CDs(D).
The particle size distributions of CDs influenced by various concentrations of NaNO3, Mg(NO3)2 and Al(NO3)3 CCDs = 15 mg/L. Effect of NaNO3(A) Effect of Mg(NO3)2 (B) Effect of Al(NO3)3(C).

Adsorption of CDs by Al2O3. The black line represents effect of cation types and concentrations on adsorption. The concentration of CDs (CCDs) is 15 mg/L, m/v = 2 g/L, pH = 7.0 and the blue line represents effect of cation types and concentrations on CDs zeta potentials, CCDs = 15 mg/L, pH = 7.0, NaCl with the concentration from 0.167 to 16.7 mM(A) CCDs = 15 mg/L, pH = 7.0, CaCl2 with the concentration from 0.2 to 1.2 mM (B) The solid line represents the concentrations effect of HA (0, 5, 10 mg/L) and the dashed line represents zeta potentials of CDs, HA and Al2O3 (CCDs = 15 mg/L, m/v = 2 g/L, CHA = 10 mg/L), pH 2.0–11. (C) Effect of temperature (303 K, 313 K, and 323 K) on adsorption. The symbols represent the experimental data, m/v = 2 g/L, pH = 7.0. Initial CDs: 6.0–42 mg/L (D). (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

The zeta potentials of minerals and CDs at various pH, CCDs = 15 mg/L, Cadsorbents = 2.0 g/L (A) The equilibrium adsorption capacity on different adsorbent (Al2O3, SiO2, kalinite and montmorillonite) without additional ionic. Cad sorbents = 2.0 g/L, T = 303 K, Time = 24 h, and pH = 3.0 ± 0.05, initial CDs: 6.0–42 mg/L (B) Csorbents = 2.0 g/L, T = 303 K, Time = 24 h, pH = 7.0 ± 0.05, initial CDs: 6.0–42 mg/L (C) Cad sorbents = 2.0 g/L, T = 303 K, Time = 24 h, pH = 10 ± 0.05, initial CDs: 6.0–42 mg/L (D).
FTIR for CDs, Al2O3 and Al2O3/CDs complex (A) SEM-EDS of CDs (B) Al2O3/CDs complex (C).
Parameters obtained from adsorption isotherm models.

ce:article-footnote.ce:note-para: This paper has been recommended for acceptance by Dr. Baoshan Xing.

head.ce:title: Adsorption of carbon dots onto Al2O3 in aqueous: Experimental and theoretical studies

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Collaborative Innovation Center of Radiation Medicine of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. Institute of Plasma Physics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, P.O. Box 1126, Hefei, 230031, PR China.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Increasing interests in carbon dots (CDs) research resulted in the increased production of CDs and the risk to be released into the environment, including surface water and groundwater. To assess the environmental behavior of CDs, the stability of CDs in aqueous solutions were first examined by different environmental conditions, followed by the systematic investigation of the adsorption behaviors of CDs onto Al2O3 surface. Electrostatic interactions were considered as the dominant forces in CDs adsorption onto Al2O3 process, which were further confirmed by controlling experiments as compared with other minerals or oxides, such as montmorillonite, kaolinite and SiO2. Theoretical calculations and characterization of Al2O3/CDs revealed chemisorptions exist in the adsorption process. Our investigation results provided fundamental understanding towards the interaction of CDs with Al2O3 surface, and potential fate of CDs under natural conditions in aquatic environment, as well as in soils and sediments.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), a new member of the carbon nanomaterial family, have drawn substantial attention due to their exceptional advantages like chemical stability, high optical absorptivity and biocompatibility (Shi et al., 2015; Baker and Baker, 2010; X. Wang et al., 2010; Sun et al., 2006; Cao et al., 2007; Kochuveedu et al., 2011). CDs were discovered by Sun and coworkers (Sun et al., 2006) as nanoscale carbon nanoparticles with simple surface passivation to exhibit strong photoluminescence in both solution and the solid state. Versatile synthetic methods were unrevealed ranging from top-down to bottom-up strategies and the application of CDs included the fields of bioimaging (Cao et al., 2007), printing inks, photocatalysis (Kochuveedu et al., 2011) and sensors (Nie et al., 2014). With the booming studies, increasing CDs were produced, which brought out the potential risks of environmental pollution by releasing into surface water or groundwater (Zhao et al., 2015). Although carbon is not regarded as an intrinsically toxic element, the specific material structures and configurations of CDs may be potential risks to human health, thereby deserving to raise public concern (Wang et al., 2013). The release of nanomaterials into the environment can occur at any stage of their life cycle (production, transportation, consumer usage, and disposal) (Zhou et al., 2012). Interactions between nanoparticle-bound ligands and cellular receptors depend on the engineered geometry and the ligand density of a nanomaterial. As a ligand's binding affinity increases proportionally to the size of nanoparticle, so the aggression of CDs may create region of highly concentrated ligand, which increases avidity and, potentially, alters cell signaling (Albanese et al., 2012). Therefore, understanding of the environmental behaviors of CDs in sediments and soils is of importance for preventing water from being contaminated by CDs and their associated contaminants.

Minerals are widely presented in aqueous solution and are important components of sediments and soils. The interaction between minerals and carbon nanoparticles have been widely reported, including various carbon materials, such as graphene/graphene oxide (GO), single-wall nanotubes (SWNTs)/multi-wall nanotubes (MWNTs), fullerenes (C60) etc, with different sizes, morphologies and properties. Owing to its excellent physiochemical properties and wide applications in various fields in the past 5 years, the fate, transport, and ecotoxicity of GO have been attracting increasing attention currently (Romanchuk et al., 2013; Ahmed and Rodrigues, 2013; Qi et al., 2014; Chowdhury et al., 2014a,b; Hou et al., 2015; Ren et al., 2014; Nguyen et al., 2017). Indranil Chowdhury et al. investigated the interactions of GO with silica surfaces using a quartz crystal microbalance with dissipation monitoring (Chowdhury et al., 2014a,b). The increasing production and application of carbon nanotubes (CNTs) also raise worldwide concerns on their potential environmental discharge and ecological risks. Jaisi et al. (2008). observed the deposition of SWNTs in porous sand media which highlighted the transport behavior of SWNTs under different environmentally relevant conditions. C60 nanoparticles, another class of carbonaceous nanomaterials can form stable nanoscale aggregates in aqueous solution (Tian et al., 2012; Li et al., 2008). And many reports have investigated the heteroaggregation behaviors and environment implications of C60 in wastewater (Choi et al., 2015; Wu et al., 2014; Snow et al., 2014; Ghosh et al., 2014). The size of nanomaterials had a great influence to their environmental migration and their interactions with environmental media (Cai et al., 2016). The effects of nanoparticle size on the deposition were attributed to the size dependent attachment rate or different level of sensitivity to the heterogeneity in the nanoparticle surface charge (May and Li, 2013; Pelley and Tufenkji, 2008). The environmental behaviors of quantum dots in size less than 10 nm, smaller than CNTs or GO, have been investigated. Examples involve the stability and removal of water soluble CdTe quantum dots in water (Zhang et al., 2007), aggregation kinetics and self-assembly mechanisms of graphene quantum dots in aqueous solutions (Li et al., 2017) and the deposition behavior of quantum dots on chemically diverse model surfaces (Quevedo et al., 2013). However, as a novel quantum dot, few investigations were reported on the interactions between CDs and natural minerals particles, which may be destabilized by minerals via possible CDs-mineral heteroaggregation.

Among the various components of geological materials, oxides and hydroxides of Ti, Al, Si, and Mn play an important role in regulating the mobility of contaminants, because of their wide spread environmental presence, high sorption capacity and tendency to form coatings on mineral surfaces (Müller et al., 2009). Al2O3 was chosen as representative natural solid particle because it can be used to model the natural Al-hydroxide phases (like gibbsite) and layers of aluminum oxide in 1:1 clay minerals (like kaolinite), which are commonly present in aquifers and soils (Liu et al., 2005). Actually, as a typical environmental media, Al2O3 has been widely used on the aggregation and deposition of active components, such as HCN, SO2, CO2, etc, both experimentally and theoretically (Kim et al., 2007; Lo et al., 2010; Pan et al., 2008).

In this study, stability of CDs in aqueous solutions by changing the storage time, pH values and ionic strengths were first examined. The adsorption of CDs onto Al2O3 surface was further investigated and the influencing factors, including ionic strength, pH and humic acid concentrations, were also considered. The formation of Al2O3/CDs complexes were confirmed by the SEM-EDS and FT-IR analysis. The adsorption mechanism was investigated by density functional theory (DFT) simulations, revealing a formation of Al-O bond between Al2O3 and the carboxylic functional groups of CDs.

CDs were synthesized through a facile, one-step hydrothermal method (Guo et al., 2013). Briefly, sodium citrate (0.2 g) and NH4HCO3 (1.5 g) (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Shanghai, China) in water (10 mL) were sealed into a Teflon-lined equipped stainless steel autoclave, which was then placed in a drying oven (DHG-9038, Shanghai Jinghong Laboratory Instrument Co., ltd) for hydrothermal treatment at 180 °C for 5 h. The purification of CDs was performed in a dialysis tube (500 Da, molecular weight cutoff) for about 24 h in dark. The concentration of the purified CDs was measured by taking aliquots for lyophilization and calculated to be 0.18 g/L. The rest purified CDs were used for batch experiments.

The adsorption experiments were performed in polyethylene test tubes by batch technique, where the stock suspensions of minerals or oxides and CDs solutions were added. In detail, 3 mL (4 g/L) of adsorbents was added to 10 mL test tubes before the addition of solution and CDs. After addition all the solution, water was used to make the volume to 6 mL. Correspondingly, the adsorption experiments were performed as a function of the ionic strength (NaCl, 0.167–16.7 mM; CaCl2 0.2–1.2 mM) at pH 7.0 with 15 mg/L of initial CDs; as a function of HA concentrations (0, 5, 10 mg/L) at pH 2.0–11.0 without electrolyte; as a function temperature (303 K, 313 K, 323 K) of CDs concentrations (6.0–42 mg/L) at a fixed equilibrium pH 7.0 ± 0.2 without electrolyte; as a function of other minerals and oxides, the concentration of adsorbents is 2 g/L, CDs concentrations (6.0–42 mg/L) at a fixed equilibrium pH 3.0 ± 0.2, 7.0 ± 0.2 and 10.0 ± 0.2 at 303 K without electrolyte. Negligible amount of 0.01–1.0 M HCl or NaOH solution was added into the suspensions to adjust pH. Then the tubes were placed in an oscillator and kept shaking for 24 h. The concentration of CDs was analyzed by UV-vis spectrophotometer. The detail information about the analysis method for CDs by UV-vis spectrophotometer was shown in the supporting information (SI).

The adsorption of CDs on minerals or oxides can be calculated from the differences between the initial (C 0) and the equilibrium concentration (C e). The adsorption was expressed in terms of adsorption percentage (%), which was calculated from the equation of
A d s o r p t i o n
% =
(
C 0
−
C e
)
/
C 0
× 100 %
(L. Wang et al., 2010). The adsorption experiments at a fixed equilibrium pH were run in duplicate, while the zeta potential analyses were run in triplicate. All the experimental data were the averages of duplicate or triplicate determinations (Huang et al., 2016). Error bars represent standard deviation.

The calculations were performed within the framework of the density functional theory (DFT) by using the Vienna ab initio simulation package (VASP). As shown in XRD, since natures of CDs are amorphous, it is difficult to build molecular models acquiring actual configurations. However, the adsorption behaviors of CDs on Al2O3 surface can be understood by exploring the bonding features between functional groups (such as -OH and -OOH) and the surface. Therefore, in current simulations, according to the characteristics of XPS and FT-IR, the finite-sized model of CDs containing reasonable functional groups is employed for investigating the interaction between CDs and Al2O3 surface. Furthermore based on the previous reports and our experiment condition, we choose a hydroxylated γ-Al2O3 (110) surface, which was modeled by a supercell with a dimension of 15.803 Å × 23.083 Å × 10.0 Å. The optimized geometries of CDs and Al2O3 were shown in Fig. S1. The adsorption energies (Ead = ECDs + EAl2O3–ECDs-Al2O3) were calculated to determine the most stable structures of CDs with Al2O3.

The XRD pattern of CDs (Fig. 1 A) showed a broad peak centered at 22° (0.2 nm) which was caused by highly disordered carbon atoms (Qu et al., 2012). In the FT-IR analysis (Fig. 1B) of CDs, typical absorption peaks were observed as the asymmetric stretching vibrations of C-NH-C at 1126 cm−1, the vibrational absorption band of C=O at 1635 cm−1, C-H vibrations at 2923 cm−1 and 2850 cm−1 and the stretching vibrations of C-OH at 3430 cm−1 (Zhu et al., 2013). The morphology and structure of CDs were also examined by TEM, UV-vis and XPS. Fig. 1C shows the TEM image of CDs, which had a uniform dispersion without obvious aggregation and the average particle diameter was 4.8 nm (Zhu et al., 2013). An absorption peak at 331 nm was detected in the UV–vis absorption spectrum (Fig. 1D). Yellow brown and bright blue colors were observed when exposed to sunlight and UV light at 365 nm, respectively (the inset pictures in Fig. 1D). It should be highlighted that the sodium citrate solution itself was non-emissive in the visible region upon excited at 331 nm (Fig. S2), suggesting that the blue fluorescence results from the as-prepared CDs (Guo et al., 2013). XPS analysis was used for the surface group detections as shown in Fig. S3B. C1s analysis demonstrated different types of carbon atoms: C=C at 284.6 eV (Shao et al., 2014), C-C at 285.9 eV, C-N at 285.5 eV, C-O at 286.6 eV, C=O at 288.3 eV (Fig. S3A) (Jia et al., 2012) indicating the co-existence of sp2 (C=C, C=O) and sp3 (C-N, C-C, C-O) carbons with a dominant sp2 carbons. With these characterization results in hand, we proposed the structure geometry of CDs for the DFT calculation (Fig. S1A).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Stability of CDs under different environmental conditions
ce:section.ce:para: Possible influencing factors, such as the storage time, CDs concentration, pH value, and ionic strength, were examined for the stabilities of CDs under different environmental conditions. For the storage time, weak UV-vis absorbance were detected at a storage time of 2 and 19 h, as shown in Fig. S4A, indicating independent stabilities of CDs with time. Similar results were obtained for the concentration effects, as depicted in Fig. S4B. pH is also an important impact factor for the stability of CDs in aqueous solutions, as examined in Fig. S4C. Three different phenomenon (color changes) were observed in the pH ranges of 2.0–3.0, 3.0–5.0 and 5.0–11.0. Therefore, three standard curves at different pH values were plotted as shown in Fig. S5. The color change can be attributed to the electronic transition changes of p–p* and n–p* by refilling or depleting their valence bands at different pH values (H. Li et al., 2012).

The particle size distributions of CDs can also be influenced by the ionic strength. At pH 8.0, the size distributions of CDs in Al3+ solution with different concentrations were investigated and shown in Fig. S6. The higher concentrations of Al3+ solutions, the faster CDs aggregated, and the z-average diameters of CDs aggregates increased up to more than 400 nm (Zhang et al., 2007). At 1.67 mM, CDs remained stable for 150 min, while the z-average diameter increased 1.6 μm within 50 min at a concentration of 3.34 mM (Fig. 2 C). At 5.01 mM, after 10 min the size of CDs became 9673 nm and sedimentation can be found, so we cannot get a balanced size diameter (Fig. S6). The observed ionic strength effects can be explained by the surface charges of CDs and Al3+ species. At pH 8.0, the dominant species distribution of Al3+ exists as negatively charged Al(OH)4 - (Fig. S7) (Ren et al., 2014), which would increase the negative potentials of CDs surface. Thus, the electronic repulsion forces between Al(OH)4 - and CDs would inhibit the bridging abilities of Al(OH)4 - to cause rapid aggregation of CDs. In addition, because amorphous Al(OH)3 (pHzpc > 8) has a positive surface charge at pH 8.0, the negative-potentials of CDs suspensions could decrease with the formation of amorphous Al(OH)3 (Cao et al., 2007). Similar ionic strength effects were also observed for the aggregation of CDs in Na+ and Mg2+ solutions at different concentrations, as collected in Fig. 2A and B. Increased particle size distribution were detected at higher concentrations.

The early stage of heteroaggregation involves the adsorption process, which has a strong or determining influence toward the aggregation efficiencies and properties, i.e. CDs-Al2O3 heteroaggregation, can be influenced by the adsorption rate constant and adsorption equilibrium (McCoy and Rolston, 1992; Febrianto et al., 2009; Doğan et al., 2004; Ho and Mckay, 1998).

Natural obtained water contains various inorganic ions, such as Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, etc, which might form a screening or shading effect on CDs surface charge (Zhao et al., 2014). The ionic strength towards the adsorption of CDs on Al2O3 was screened and plotted in Fig. 3 at different concentrations of electrolytes, NaCl and CaCl2. The corresponding zeta potentials of CDs as a function of NaCl and CaCl2 concentrations were measured and displayed in Fig. 3A and B. The adsorption efficiencies increased with increasing concentrations of electrolytes and bivalent cations (Ca2+) had higher adsorption capacity than the monovalent cation (Na+) on the same concentration. This observation can be explained by the surface zeta potentials of Al2O3. An increased cation concentration would enhance the charge neutralization effect by cations, resulting in increased surface zeta potentials of CDs. Higher surface zeta potentials would be obtained by the bivalent cations (Ca2+) than the monovalent cation (Na+) (Kim et al., 2007). Meanwhile, an increased zeta potential of Al2O3 would decrease the potentials gaps between CDs and Al2O3, resulting in increased electrostatic attractions and the increased adsorption efficiency. This phenomenon suggested an electrostatic interaction controlled adsorption process.

Humic acid (HA) is polyelectrolytic macromolecules that are widely distributed in natural waters, which can exhibit strong complexation abilities by interacting with metal ions (Yakout et al., 2013). The humic acid effect toward CDs adsorption on Al2O3 is also investigated at different pH values and concentrations (Fig. 3C).

At pH < 5.0, high adsorption efficiencies were still obtained at both concentrations, which can be explained by the strong complexation abilities between the positively charged Al2O3 and negatively charged HA and CDs, resulting in the adsorption of both HA and CDs on Al2O3 surfaces and little HA effects at both concentration (J. Li et al., 2012). At higher pH values, the surface of Al2O3 become negative, therefore the adsorption capacities decrease. Furthermore, in high pH the concentration of HA has more effect on the adsorption CDs capacity because the negative charge surface in Al2O3 in high pH lead to limited capacity. HA-Al2O3 complexes were formed as higher concentrations of HA were added, providing more available HA sites (Floroiu et al., 2001; Fu and Zhu, 2013). The fact that fewer HA molecules are adsorbed on Al2O3 (i.e., there are more HA molecules in the solution) indicates that there is less complexation of CDs with adsorbed HA on Al2O3. The adsorption of CDs therefore decreases with increasing pH in the presence of HA. This result further confirmed the electrostatic interaction controlled adsorption process.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Adsorption isotherm for CDs on Al2O3
ce:section.ce:para:
The adsorption isotherms provided fundamental understandings in the adsorption mode of an adsorbate on adsorbent surface once the equilibrium are attained. The isotherms were fitted using Langmuir (q
e =bq
max
C
e
/(1+bC
e ) and Freundlich (q
e =K
F
C
e
n ) (Fig. 3D) (Zhao et al., 2015), where C
e (mg/L) is the equilibrium concentration of CDs in aqueous solution, q
e (mg/g) is the amount of CDs sorbed on Al2O3, q max (mg/g) is the maximum amount of CDs sorbed per unit weight of Al2O3 to form a complete monolayer coverage on the surface, and KF (mg1−nLn/g) and n are the Freundlich constants related to the adsorption capacity and adsorption intensity, respectively. Based on the calculated R2 values (Table 1 ), the Freundlich isotherm model fits better than Langmuir model. The adsorption of CDs onto Al2O3 was a favorable process because all of the estimated n values were less than unity (n < 1). Freundlich isotherms can result from overlapping of several Langmuir isotherms (combination of Langmuir isotherms) that described adsorption CDs onto Al2O3 consisting of several different sites. Fig. 3D also showed the influence of temperature on CDs adsorption onto Al2O3. Clearly, the experimental data suggested that temperature did not play significant role in CDs attachment onto Al2O3. The results can give a guide to CDs adsorption onto Al2O3 on different temperature as the environment temperature always change in a day.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Interactions mechanism at the CDs-Al2O3 surface
ce:section.ce:para: To further corroborate the electrostatic interaction controlled adsorption process, two minerals (montmorillonite and kaolinite) and a common oxide (SiO2) were introduced to compare the adsorption behaviors with Al2O3, as collected in Fig. 4 . The rationale design was based on the surface charges at different pH values (Fig. 4A). To be specifically, montmorillonite, SiO2 and CDs had negatively charged surfaces at all pH ranges, while the surface charges changed from positive to negative for kalinite and Al2O3 with increasing pH values. The pH values at point of zero charges were 3.8 and 8.9 for kalinite and Al2O3, respectively. In this case, it was expected that montmorillonite and SiO2 would exhibit very weak adsorption efficiencies at all pH values. As for kalinite and Al2O3, higher adsorption efficiencies would be expected at low pH values (pH = 3.0) and an increasing pH value would decrease the adsorption efficiencies.

Three pH values (3.0, 7.0 and 10.0) were selected to investigate the adsorption behaviors, as illustrated in Fig. 4B, C and D, respectively. At pH 3.0 (Fig. 4B), the adsorption hierarchical order was kaolinite > Al2O3 with equilibrium adsorption capacities from 22 to 15 mg/g, montmorillonite and SiO2 had nearly no adsorption. This observation was as expected due to the electrostatic interactions of the negatively charged CDs with positively charged kaolinite and Al2O3. When pH increased to 7.0, the surface charge of kaolinite changed from positive to negative, and a weak adsorption was also observed (Fig. 4C). The adsorption behaviors of Al2O3 towards CDs were still observed, but with lower equilibrium capacities (13-12 mg/g). A further increment of pH value to 10.0 resulted in all negatively charged surfaces. However, weak adsorption capacities (4-3 mg/g) were still observed for Al2O3, which might be attributed to the Lewis acid-base interactions and the hydrogen bonding effects. Specifically, given the abundant oxygen-containing groups on the surface of CDs (Yang et al., 2013), strong hydrogen-bonding interactions can be formed with hydroxyl groups on the Al2O3 particle surface (Yakout et al., 2013). Besides hydrogen-bonding interaction, Lewis acid-base interactions can also be formed (Yang et al., 2013). The hydroxyl groups from Al(OH)4 - can be regarded as a Lewis base because of the lone electron pairs on the oxygen atoms, whereas numerous Lewis acid centers occur on CDs because of the withdrawal of electron density by the more electronegative oxygen groups in CDs (Gonçalves et al., 2011). The overall hydrogen bonding effects and Lewis acid-base interactions overcome the charge repulsion, resulting in adsorption of CDs on Al2O3 surfaces. Similar phenomenon was also observed on the flocculation mechanism of GO and mineral from water by Yang et al. (2013).

The adsorption experiments showed electrostatic interaction dominated the adsorption process. However, whether chemisorptions exist in the adsorption process or not was unknown. Characterization of Al2O3/CDs complexes and theoretical calculations by DFT were used to discuss this question.

FT-IR spectra further confirmed the successful formation of Al2O3/CDs complexes by detecting both functional groups of Al2O3 and CDs shown in Fig. 5 A and new bonds found in the results which confirm chemisorptions can exist in the adsorption process. The EDS analyses of Al2O3 and Al2O3/CDs complexes were shown in Fig. 5B and C. The atomic ratios of Al2O3 were calculated to be 23.48 and 76.32% for Al and O elements, respectively, with about 0.2% silica observed, which could be attributed to the interference from the silica template. After adsorption, 18% carbon element was observed on the surfaces of Al2O3/CDs, suggesting the successful adsorption of CDs by Al2O3.

To understand the adsorption mechanism, DFT calculations were applied to clarify possible chemical or physical interactions between CDs and Al2O3 surface, such as π-π interaction, hydrophobic effect, electrostatic interaction (Coulomb force), hydrogen bonding, Lewis acid-base interaction, and so on (Zhao et al., 2014). The optimized structures of Al2O3/CDs complexes are shown in Fig. S8. The most stable adsorption structure of Al2O3 and CDs can be obtained by combining Al atom with three –COOH groups of CDs with the bond lengths of 2.039 Å, 2.127 Å and 2.214 Å. The strong surface complexation forces can be attributed to the new formed Al-O bonds from the carboxylic functional groups on the surfaces of CDs, indicating a chemisorption process. The binding energies of the Al2O3/CDs complexes were calculated in favorable binding energies of 1.63 eV. The results of FTIR, SEM analysis, and DFT calculations indicate that chemisorptions exist in the process while electrostatic interaction dominated the adsorption process.

A clear understanding of the principles in controlling CDs aggregation is crucial to predict their transportation and fate in water environment. Both batch experiments and DFT calculations revealed the critical roles of electrostatic interactions in the adsorption of CDs with minerals or oxides. Other possible interactions, such as hydrogen bonding effects and Lewis acid-base interactions were also revealed. Our investigation results provided fundamental understandings toward the interactions between CDs and Al2O3 and would partly help us to assess its environmental impact.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Financial supports from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21677146), the special scientific research fund of public welfare profession of China (201509074) and the Jiangsu Provincial Key Laboratory of Radiation Medicine and Protection and the Priority Academic Program Development of Jiangsu Higher Education Institutions are acknowledged.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supporting information available contains 8 figures (Figs. S1–S8).

The following is the supplementary data related to this article:
Online-data Online-data

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data related to this article can be found at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.envpol.2017.04.041.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Investigation of acute effects of graphene oxide on wastewater microbial community: a case study
The effect of nanoparticle size, shape, and surface chemistry on biological systems
Effect of different-sized colloids on the transport and deposition of titanium dioxide nanoparticles in quartz sand
Interaction of C60 with water: first-principles modeling and environmental implications
Interactions of graphene oxide nanomaterials with natural organic matter and metal oxide surfaces
Deposition and release of graphene oxide nanomaterials using a quartz crystal microbalance
Kinetics and mechanism of removal of methylene blue by adsorption onto perlite
Equilibrium and kinetic studies in adsorption of heavy metals using biosorbent: a summary of recent studies
Cadmium adsorption on aluminum oxide in the presence of polyacrylic acid
Graphene oxide-facilitated reduction of nitrobenzene in sulfide-containing aqueous solutions
Binary short-range colloidal assembly of magnetic iron oxides nanoparticles and fullerene (nC60) in environmental media
Ammonia removal using activated carbons: effect of the surface chemistry in dry and moist conditions
Hydrothermal synthesis of highly fluorescent carbon nanoparticles from sodium citrate and their use for the detection of mercury ions
Sorption of dye from aqueous solution by peat
Photochemical transformation of graphene oxide in sunlight
Effect of co-existing kaolinite and goethite on the aggregation of graphene oxide in the aquatic environment
Transport of single-walled carbon nanotubes in porous media: filtration mechanisms and reversibility
